title: Aspergillosis in chicks: recognition and response
subtitle: The fungal lung disease most likely to kill a brooder batch.
excerpt: Aspergillosis is a fungal infection of the respiratory tract caused by Aspergillus fumigatus spores in contaminated bedding or feed. It kills chicks in the first two to three weeks of life and has no effective field treatment once established. Prevention through clean, dry bedding is the whole game.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Meat Chickens and Broiler Breeders (2018). Merck Veterinary Manual, aspergillosis section (online edition). RSPCA factsheet on poultry respiratory disease.

glossaryTerms.definition: A fungal disease caused by Aspergillus fumigatus spores inhaled by young birds. It forms yellow-green nodules in the lungs and air sacs. Young chicks under three weeks old are most vulnerable; adult birds usually resist infection unless immune-compromised.

content.text: is the most serious disease risk in the first three weeks of a brooder batch. Affected chicks die suddenly or show progressive breathlessness, open-mouth breathing, and neck extension before death. By the time symptoms appear, lung damage is already extensive. Survival rates with established infection are low.

Aspergillus spores are everywhere but cause disease only when chicks breathe in a large dose. The main sources are mouldy bedding, unclean hatcher trays, and feed that has got damp and clumped. A single mouldy bale of shavings in a brooder can infect an entire batch.
Rapid unexpected deaths in chicks aged one to three weeks with no other explanation
Open-mouth breathing, gasping, or neck-stretching in several chicks at the same time
Chicks that lag behind littermates and squat away from the heat source
What to do if you suspect aspergillosis

Remove all bedding and replace with fresh, dry shavings from a sealed bag. Move surviving chicks to a clean space if possible. There is no reliable on-farm treatment once respiratory aspergillosis is established. Antifungal drugs exist but require a vet prescription and work poorly in young chicks. The priority is stopping new infections. Submit a dead chick to your vet for post-mortem if the cause is unclear; yellow-green nodules in the lungs are diagnostic.
Use fresh shavings from a sealed bale. Never use straw bales that smell musty or show any discolouration.
Disinfect and dry the incubator between every batch, including the hatcher tray and the fan blade.
Store feed in sealed containers; discard any pellets that have absorbed moisture or clumped.
Brooder ventilation matters. High humidity and ammonia from damp bedding make lung damage from spores worse. Fresh air flow and dry bedding work together.
